title: fix: passing `chalk review` never clears a needs:review block — the printed guidance omits `chalk unblock`, stranding the task

acceptanceCriteria.text: A passing `chalk review` on a task blocked with `needs:review` auto-unblocks it (restore `blockedFrom`, clear `t.block`), or the review-blocked guidance in next/status/backlog explicitly includes the `chalk unblock` step
Locked test drives the full round trip: run-loop review block → fix → `chalk review` pass → task is runnable again without manual state surgery
`chalk done` succeeds after that round trip (no residual blocked-state gate failure)
A passing chalk review (adversarial or manual) on a task blocked with needs:review auto-unblocks it: restores blockedFrom, clears t.block, and announces it — so runnableTasks stops skipping it.
A non-review block (needs: human-input/creds/decision/upstream) is a real dependency and is NEVER cleared by a passing review.
Locked test drives the full round trip: run-loop review block → fix → chalk review pass → task runnable again → chalk done succeeds, with no manual state surgery.

issue.body:
The run loop auto-blocks a refuted task with `needs:review` (lib/run.mjs:119), and `chalk next`/`chalk status` tell the agent to 'fix the findings, then `chalk review <id>`' (bin/chalk.mjs:180, 1075). But the `review` command (bin/chalk.mjs:1417-1471) never touches `t.state`/`t.block`, and nothing else unblocks on a pass — so a task whose re-review PASSES stays `state=blocked`, `runnableTasks` keeps skipping it, and an agent following the printed guidance exactly can end the sweep with a green-reviewed task still parked. Reviewer flagged the unverified unblock→re-review round trip on #116.

- [ ] A passing `chalk review` on a task blocked with `needs:review` auto-unblocks it (restore `blockedFrom`, clear `t.block`), or the review-blocked guidance in next/status/backlog explicitly includes the `chalk unblock` step
- [ ] Locked test drives the full round trip: run-loop review block → fix → `chalk review` pass → task is runnable again without manual state surgery
- [ ] `chalk done` succeeds after that round trip (no residual blocked-state gate failure)

_filed by `chalk retro` (self-healing)_

findings.note: The supplied diff contains only .chalk bookkeeping (PR metadata in board/tasks/updates); the actual behavior change lives in the working tree at bin/chalk.mjs (clearReviewBlockOnPass, lines 73-80, wired into both the adversarial path line 1479 and the manual --note path line 1455). Inspected the tree per instructions — the implementation is present and correct, not missing.

All six criteria are satisfied: clearReviewBlockOnPass only fires when t.block?.needs==='review', restoring t.state from t.blockedFrom (set by `block` at line 1201) and deleting block/blockedFrom; it is invoked only on verdict==='pass'. A non-review block (needs:human-input/creds/etc.) is correctly left intact. Both paths announce '✓ needs:review block cleared — task is runnable again' (lines 1460/1487), matching the test's /runnable again|unblock|cleared/i assertion.

The locked test drives the full round trip (run-loop review block → fix → review pass → runnable → done), the manual --note path, and the negative case (human-input block NOT cleared). Reverting clearReviewBlockOnPass would leave state=blocked and fail assertions at lines 51-53/74-75 — not a vacuous pass. Coverage is adequate for every criterion including the non-review guard.

title: fix: issue-intake spine writes leak into unrelated task branches — recurring scoped-diff review noise

acceptanceCriteria.text: Issue import commits its spine changes (tasks.json entries, board rows) to the base branch in a dedicated chore(spine) commit before any task branch is cut, or the task commit stage excludes spine entries not belonging to the current task
A task branch's committed diff contains no tasks.json/board entries for other tasks imported during the sweep
Test: fixture runs intake → branch → commit and asserts the task commit touches only the current task's spine entry

The adversarial reviewer's captured diff excludes spine STATE (tasks.json, chalk.json, updates.jsonl, questions.json, decisions.md, lessons.md, boards/, plans/, handoffs/, analysis/) via git pathspec exclusions, so intake's imported-task and board churn never reaches the reviewer.
Contract artifacts stay visible to the reviewer: the code change and pinned .chalk/tests/ e2e specs (and .chalk/evidence/) are NOT excluded.
The exclusion applies to every captureDiff fallback (git diff HEAD, base...HEAD, origin/base...HEAD, git diff) and to the --stat file list, in both manual and pipeline order.
Locked test proves the reviewer's prompt carries the code + a .chalk/tests/ spec but not tasks.json/board rows/other imported task titles.

issue.body:
Four reviews in this sweep (release --promote, release --commit recovery, review-stage fix, cost ledger) each flagged the same design-intent finding: the task's diff bundles unrelated queue metadata — imported issue task specs and board rows written during intake — violating chalk's own 'keep diffs small and scoped' hard rule and burning reviewer attention every run. Intake mutates .chalk/tasks.json/board while that state is later swept into whichever task branch commits next.

- [ ] Issue import commits its spine changes (tasks.json entries, board rows) to the base branch in a dedicated chore(spine) commit before any task branch is cut, or the task commit stage excludes spine entries not belonging to the current task
- [ ] A task branch's committed diff contains no tasks.json/board entries for other tasks imported during the sweep
- [ ] Test: fixture runs intake → branch → commit and asserts the task commit touches only the current task's spine entry

_filed by `chalk retro` (self-healing)_

findings.note: Criteria 1 & 2 are unmet. The task contract requires the ROOT-cause fix: issue import commits spine changes in a dedicated chore(spine) commit before task branches are cut, OR the task-commit stage excludes non-current-task spine entries — and 'A task branch's committed diff contains no tasks.json/board entries for other tasks.' The change touches only lib/review.mjs captureDiff; it masks spine churn from the REVIEWER's view but leaves the actual committed branch diff exactly as leaky as before. The committed history still bundles unrelated imported-task/board entries, so criterion 2 (committed diff) is not satisfied.

Criterion 3 mandates a test that 'runs intake → branch → commit and asserts the task commit touches only the current task's spine entry.' No such test exists — the only locked test (review-diff-scope.test.mjs) exercises the reviewer prompt, never intake/branch/commit. The commit-scoping behavior demanded by criteria 1-3 ships with zero test coverage (vacuous pass).

Criterion 6 requires the exclusion to hold 'in both manual and pipeline order.' The test only covers manual mode (uncommitted tasks.json via git diff HEAD). The pipeline path — change committed on-branch so git diff HEAD is empty and it falls through to `git diff <base>...HEAD` — is never asserted; that fallback would still pass if the pathspec were reverted for that branch.

The stated goal (issue #114) is that spine writes leak into unrelated task branches, violating 'keep diffs small and scoped.' Hiding the leak from the adversarial reviewer solves the symptom (review noise) while leaving the underlying scoped-diff violation in the committed branches intact — arguably solving the wrong problem relative to criteria 1-3.

The intake commit stages spine files with `gitAdd(spineFiles)` but then calls `gitCommit`, which runs a bare `git commit -m ...` (lib/git.mjs:77-79) with NO pathspec — it commits the ENTIRE staged index. If a user has ANY pre-staged changes when they run `chalk issue pull` (staged but not yet committed code), those changes are swept into the auto-generated `chore(spine)` commit on the base branch. This directly contradicts the code's own explicit safety claim ("Scoped: only the spine files intake touched, never `git add -A`, so a user's other uncommitted work is untouched"). The `diff --cached --name-only` guard only checks whether the index is non-empty, then commits all of it. This is a real workflow/data hazard: unrelated user work gets committed to base under a misleading message.

intake-commit-scope.test.mjs only exercises a clean index before `issue pull` (test 1 asserts the commit touches only `.chalk/` files, but the repo has nothing else staged). No test stages an unrelated file before intake to prove the scoping guarantee the comment makes. The 'touches ONLY spine files, never code' assertion would still pass in the tested scenario even though the implementation would sweep pre-staged code in a real one — the test does not actually exercise the scoping claim's failure mode.

Criterion 1 requires the intake commit before any task branch is cut; this holds in worktree-disabled mode (the tests force `worktree.enabled=false`), but with worktrees enabled by default the interaction of committing to base at pull time vs. later branch creation is not exercised by any locked test, leaving the default-mode path unverified.

Criterion 6 requires the pathspec exclusion to apply to every captureDiff fallback (git diff HEAD, base...HEAD, origin/base...HEAD, git diff) in both manual and pipeline order. review-diff-scope.test.mjs only exercises the manual 'git diff HEAD' path (uncommitted staged changes); the pipeline path (committed-on-branch base...HEAD / origin/base...HEAD) is not directly asserted. Coverage is not vacuous, however: exclusions are a single shared `diffPathspec` constant appended to all four fallbacks and the --stat, and the REVIEW_DIFF_EXCLUDES unit test plus the integration test both fail if the exclusion is reverted.

The intake commit's spineFiles list (.chalk/tasks.json, updates.jsonl, boards, plans) is a subset of the reviewer's exclude list (which also covers chalk.json, questions.json, decisions.md, lessons.md, handoffs, analysis). If a future intake path dirties one of those un-committed spine files, it would float in the working tree again. Currently benign — intake only writes tasks.json/updates.jsonl/boards, and the locked test asserts a clean tree — but the two lists diverging is a latent maintenance hazard.

`chalk issue pull` now auto-creates a chore(spine) commit on the current (base) branch where it previously left changes uncommitted. Behavior is intended per criterion 1 and best-effort guarded (rev-parse check + try/catch, empty-commit skip, partial `git commit -- <pathspec>` leaving user's pre-staged work intact, all covered by intake-commit-scope tests incl. the non-git and pre-staged cases), but callers/pipelines that assumed intake left the spine uncommitted now see a new base-branch commit.

title: fix: chalk spec --test from a linked worktree records a '../<worktree>/…' lock path — dead after cleanup

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk spec --test <path> and chalk amend-spec --test <path> invoked from a linked git worktree record the lock 'path' relative to the worktree's copy of the project root (tree-relative, e.g. test/x.mjs) — never a '../<worktree>/…' path that points into the worktree and dies after chalk merge cleans it up

The recorded tree-relative path resolves under the canonical spine root as well (survives worktree cleanup): store.brokenLocks against the canonical root finds the file when it exists there and reports no false integrity break
From the canonical root (not inside a linked worktree) the recorded lock path is unchanged — still relative to the spine root — so no existing lock is rewritten; a monorepo subdir offset is preserved

issue.body:
## What happened

Dogfooding on `chalkagents/chalk-protocol-site` (issue #6 there): the reviewer blocked with "the test is not registered as a locked test", so — per the finding — I ran the sanctioned path from the task's **worktree** (so the file exists at the cwd):

```
cd ../chalk-protocol-site-feat-6-…   # the task worktree
chalk spec task-13052f3 --test test/docs-pages.test.mjs
  locked ../chalk-protocol-site-feat-6-feat-build-time-docs-rendering/test/docs-pages.test.mjs @ 15892a308f4b
```

The lock recorded `path: "../chalk-protocol-site-feat-6-…/test/docs-pages.test.mjs"` — relative to the canonical spine root (the #52 single-canonical-spine resolution works), but pointing INTO the worktree. After `chalk merge` cleans the worktree up, the recorded path is dead: integrity/break-it checks against it will ENOENT even though `test/docs-pages.test.mjs` exists on main.

## Where

`bin/chalk.mjs` spec/amend-spec: `s.lockTest(resolve(process.cwd(), String(p)))` — the abs path is later relativized against `store.root` (main checkout), so a cwd inside a linked worktree produces a `../<worktree>/…` path.

## Expected

When cwd is a linked worktree of the canonical root, the lock path should be recorded relative to the **worktree root** (i.e. `test/docs-pages.test.mjs`), which is the tree-relative path valid in every checkout. `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` from cwd (or comparing against `workdir(store, task)`) gives the right base.

## Workaround used

Normalized the recorded path by hand (same sha) + `chalk decision` documenting it.

## Related

The same dogfood run also reproduced #102's stage-marker friction in a new spot: after a manual review-fix round (manual commit+push, review PASS), the resumed `chalk pipeline` re-ran `work` (executor no-op, attempts++) and then FAILED at `commit` with "nothing to commit — the executor made no file changes", auto-blocking a task whose PR was already pushed, recorded, and LGTM'd. A clean tree + an existing recorded PR should advance the stage, not block. Happy to split this into its own issue if it shouldn't ride on #102.

findings.note: Criterion 3's monorepo-subdir clause is only exercised for the trivial case where the project root equals the git top-level (test 2 uses no subdir). The new offset computation (`offset = relative(wt.mainRoot, this.root)` then `resolve(wt.toplevel, offset)`) only does non-trivial work when a chalk project lives in a monorepo subdir INSIDE a linked worktree, and no test covers that path. The logic reads correct (offset direction main→worktree yields tree-relative), but it ships unverified.

lockBase relies on `this.root` already being the canonical MAIN-checkout project root (via findRoot's worktree→main redirect). In a monorepo worktree without a committed .chalk, findRoot may fail to redirect and this.root would be the worktree's own root, making `offset` degenerate; the redirect+lockBase interaction is not jointly tested. Non-blocking as the tested non-monorepo flow works end-to-end.

The core changed behavior (worktree cwd → tree-relative lock path, no '../<worktree>/…') is asserted by test 1 with assertions that fail on revert (path=='test/foo.test.mjs', !startsWith('..'), !includes(worktree basename)) plus brokenLocks==[] under the canonical root, so criteria 1 and 2 are non-vacuously covered.

title: feat: parallel task execution — scope P6 integrity per worktree, lock spine writes, fan out the driver

acceptanceCriteria.text: verify() checks each in-progress task's locked tests against THAT task's own worktree (task.worktree, falling back to store.root) instead of one shared cwd — so a second in-progress task on its own branch no longer trips a false P6 integrity break in the first task's checkout
Single in-progress task behavior is unchanged: when the task's worktree equals the verify cwd (the common case), the integrity result is byte-identical to today

The opt-in all-locks DONE-task integrity check still runs against the verify cwd (the current worktree), preserving the #80 anti-cheat that catches the current task weakening an already-done task's locked test

issue.body:
**From the 2026-07-06 dogfood sweeps.** Chalk's one-in-progress-task-at-a-time is a soft convention with one hard tooth, and it forces whole-repo sequential sweeps even when tasks are independent.

## What actually enforces sequential today

- `chalk start` does NOT refuse a second in-progress task; `chalk next` only warns (`protocol prefers ONE at a time`).
- The hard tooth is **P6**: `verify()` (lib/verify.mjs:39) checks EVERY in-progress task's locked tests against a SINGLE cwd. Two tasks in-progress on different branches → each checkout is missing the other task's locked test (or has a stale sha) → `brokenLocks` fires → verify RED for both. Worktrees isolate the *edits* but not the *integrity check*.
- The `chalk verify` CLI picks the FIRST in-progress task (`tasks().find(...)`, bin/chalk.mjs) to choose the worktree — wrong cwd for any second task.
- The Store writes the spine with plain `writeFileSync` — two concurrent chalk processes in the same repo race on tasks.json/updates.jsonl (last writer wins, events can interleave mid-line).
- `runDriver`/`runPipeline` are sequential for-loops over the queue.

(Parallel across DIFFERENT repos already works fine — each spine is independent.)

## Sketch

1. **Scope P6 per task**: check each in-progress task's locked tests in *its own* worktree (task.worktree, falling back to root). A task without a worktree in multi-mode is the error case.
2. **Spine write safety**: a lockfile (or O_APPEND-only event log + per-task json) so concurrent `chalk work`/`verify`/`done` don't clobber tasks.json.
3. **Driver fan-out**: `chalk pipeline --parallel N` — spawn per-task stage chains (branch→work→…→merge) in their own worktrees; merges still serialize through the gate (they contend on the base branch anyway).
4. Make the one-at-a-time preference explicit: `chalk start` refuses a second wip task unless `protocol.parallel.enabled` (or `--parallel`), instead of today's silent allow + downstream RED.

Until then the documented answer stays: one task in flight per repo, queue the rest (`chalk next` order).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

findings.note: workdir() falls back to store.root, so an in-progress task with NO worktree field verified under an explicit non-root cwd would now hash at store.root instead of cwd — a behavior change vs. today. But this only diverges outside criterion 2's stated condition (worktree==cwd) and the pipeline always sets task.worktree when running in a worktree, so it's benign; not a violation of the contract.

Criterion 2 (single in-progress task, worktree==cwd → byte-identical) is not asserted by a dedicated test; it is only implied by the workdir fallback. However tests 1-3 each map to and discriminate criteria 1, genuine-break, and 3, and test 1 would fail if the change were reverted, so the core behavior change is covered.
Change is minimal, scoped, and directly implements per-worktree P6 scoping while keeping the #80 done-task all-locks anti-cheat at cwd (line 57). Matches the stated goal.

title: fix: chalk commit silently no-ops after the first commit, so review-fix changes never get committed

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk commit commits new working-tree code changes even past the 'committed' stage (labeled follow-up), so review-fix changes land in git.
It no-ops idempotently when there is nothing new; the first commit still errors when the executor produced no changes.
After a follow-up commit no uncommitted CODE lingers — reviewer's view equals what merge takes.
Locked test proves first commit, follow-up landing, idempotent re-run, and empty-first-commit failure.

issue.body:
**Discovered while landing #114/#125.** `chalk commit` guards on `stageDone(t, 'committed')` and returns `ok('commit (already done)')` if the task's pipeline stage is at/after `committed`. But the review→fix loop routinely makes MORE code changes AFTER the first commit (to address a review BLOCK). On the next `chalk commit`, it no-ops — the fix changes stay uncommitted in the working tree.

**Real failure:** #114's review-round-2 fix (gitCommitPaths + intake commit + scoping test) was made after the first commit. `chalk commit` no-op'd; I committed only the spine manually; the code fix never reached the fix/114 branch and was squash-merged away. #114 shipped with only its round-1 half. The adversarial review PASSED because captureDiff shows uncommitted working-tree changes (`git diff HEAD`), so the reviewer saw the fix — but the MERGE only took committed changes. Required a manual split (#133 + rescoped #132) to recover.

**Fix options:**
- `chalk commit` should re-commit new working-tree changes when the branch has uncommitted code (amend or a follow-up commit), not silently no-op; OR
- loudly WARN when `chalk commit` is a no-op but the working tree has uncommitted code changes ('N files changed since the last commit — git add + commit them, or they won't land').

The mismatch between what the reviewer sees (working tree) and what merge takes (committed) is the deeper hazard — a green review can certify code that never lands.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: refactor: unify the intake-commit spine paths and the reviewer diff-exclude list into one shared constant

acceptanceCriteria.text: Define the spine-state path list once in a shared module imported by both bin/chalk.mjs (intake commit) and lib/review.mjs (diff excludes)
Ensure the intake commit covers every spine path the reviewer excludes (contract artifacts like `.chalk/tests/` and `.chalk/evidence/` remain visible/committed as today)
Add a test asserting the intake-commit path set and the reviewer-exclude set stay consistent, so a future divergence trips the suite

issue.body:
`chalk issue pull` commits a hardcoded `spineFiles` list (`.chalk/tasks.json`, `.chalk/updates.jsonl`, `.chalk/boards`, `.chalk/plans`) in bin/chalk.mjs:337, while the reviewer excludes a *superset* of spine paths via `REVIEW_DIFF_EXCLUDES` in lib/review.mjs:66 (also `.chalk/chalk.json`, `.chalk/questions.json`, `.chalk/decisions.md`, `.chalk/lessons.md`, `.chalk/handoffs`, `.chalk/analysis`).

Because the intake list is a subset, any future intake path that dirties a spine file NOT in `spineFiles` (e.g. `chalk.json` or `questions.json`) is left uncommitted by intake yet excluded from the review diff — so it floats in the working tree and bundles into the next task branch, re-introducing exactly the scoped-diff leak #114 fixed. The two lists silently diverging is a latent maintenance hazard the #130 review flagged.

- [ ] Define the spine-state path list once in a shared module imported by both bin/chalk.mjs (intake commit) and lib/review.mjs (diff excludes)
- [ ] Ensure the intake commit covers every spine path the reviewer excludes (contract artifacts like `.chalk/tests/` and `.chalk/evidence/` remain visible/committed as today)
- [ ] Add a test asserting the intake-commit path set and the reviewer-exclude set stay consistent, so a future divergence trips the suite

_filed by `chalk retro` (self-healing)_

findings.note: Intake now commits additional spine paths not previously committed (chalk.json, questions.json, decisions.md, lessons.md, handoffs, analysis). If a user has manually-edited uncommitted `.chalk/chalk.json` at `chalk issue pull` time, those config edits would now be swept into the chore(spine) commit via `git commit -- <paths>`. This is consistent with the stated 'chalk owns spine state' intent, but is a behavior change not directly exercised by a runtime test (only tasks.json/boards/updates are actually written during pull in the fixtures).

The consistency test (test/spine-paths-consistency.test.mjs) pins REVIEW_DIFF_EXCLUDES == SPINE_STATE_PATHS wrapped, which is somewhat tautological given review.mjs literally maps the constant — its real value is the source-scan guard forbidding a re-hardcoded `.chalk/` list on the spineFiles line. No test functionally asserts that intake commits e.g. questions.json/chalk.json specifically; coverage rests on the structural constant-sharing plus the pre-existing 'working tree clean after intake' check. Adequate but indirect for criterion 2.

title: fix: untrackedLockedTests compares pinned paths verbatim against git ls-files — a './'-prefixed, backslashed, or case-differing pin false-blocks done/pr

acceptanceCriteria.text: Normalize both the pinned path and each `ls-files` entry before comparison (strip a leading `./`, unify path separators) so equivalent forms match
Handle case-insensitive filesystems so a case-only difference does not false-block (or document the platform boundary)
Add a locked test: a tracked file pinned as `./<path>` (and a backslash/case variant) is NOT reported untracked and `chalk done` proceeds
Keep the genuinely-untracked case still blocking (no regression to the #107 vacuous-green guard)

issue.body:
`untrackedLockedTests` (lib/testgate.mjs:65) does `pinned.filter(p => !tracked.has(p))` where `tracked` is the raw `git ls-files -z` output. Because it is exact string equality with no normalization, a locked test whose pinned path is written as `./contract.test.mjs`, uses backslashes on Windows, or differs only in case on a case-insensitive filesystem is reported as untracked even though git tracks it — a false block on `chalk done`/`chalk pr` that strands an otherwise-shippable task. The tracking gate should only ever fire on a genuinely untracked file.

- [ ] Normalize both the pinned path and each `ls-files` entry before comparison (strip a leading `./`, unify path separators) so equivalent forms match
- [ ] Handle case-insensitive filesystems so a case-only difference does not false-block (or document the platform boundary)
- [ ] Add a locked test: a tracked file pinned as `./<path>` (and a backslash/case variant) is NOT reported untracked and `chalk done` proceeds
- [ ] Keep the genuinely-untracked case still blocking (no regression to the #107 vacuous-green guard)

_filed by `chalk retro` (self-healing)_

findings.note: Criterion 3 names a backslash variant; the end-to-end `chalk done` proceeds path is only asserted for `./`-prefix and case variants. Backslash is covered only via the normTrackPath unit assertion (normTrackPath('./a\\b.mjs')===normTrackPath('a/b.mjs')). Acceptable since untrackedLockedTests normalizes both sides through that same shared helper, but no full-loop backslash assertion exists (a POSIX git tree can't track a backslash-separated file, so this is inherently unit-level).

Backslash→'/' unification is unconditional even on POSIX, where a literal backslash is a valid filename character; a pinned `a\b` could then spuriously match a tracked `a/b`, weakening the #107 guard in that pathological case. Extremely unlikely in practice for pinned test paths, so non-blocking.

case-insensitivity keys solely off `git config core.ignorecase`. If a user's repo has it unset/false on an ignorecase FS, a case-only pin would still false-block; the change documents this as the platform boundary (criterion 2 permits documentation), so acceptable. Revert-sensitivity confirmed: the `./`-pin and case tests both fail if the normalization is reverted, so the change is genuinely covered.

title: feat: spine write safety — atomic tasks.json writes + append-only event log so concurrent chalk processes don't clobber the spine (#110 slice 2)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Concurrent chalk processes mutating tasks.json via upsertTask do not lose updates: the read-modify-write runs under a cross-process advisory lock and RE-READS inside it, so N concurrent adds of distinct tasks all survive (no last-writer-wins clobber)
Spine JSON writes are atomic (temp file + rename): a concurrent reader always sees a complete valid file, and no .tmp residue is left after a successful write

The lock self-heals and stays out of the repo: a crashed holder's stale lock (older than the threshold) is stolen so a dead process cannot wedge the spine; the .lock and atomic-write temp files are gitignored

findings.note: Criterion 3 (lock self-heals: a stale lock older than LOCK_STALE_MS is stolen) has NO test in the locked suite. The entire stale-steal branch in withLock (statSync mtime > LOCK_STALE_MS → unlinkSync → continue) could be deleted and both locked tests would still pass. A crashed-holder scenario is never simulated. Feature shipped with no covering assertion — vacuous pass per the hard rule.

Criterion 3 also requires the .lock and atomic-write temp files be gitignored. No test asserts .gitignore ignores .chalk/.lock or the tasks.json.<pid>.tmp temp pattern. The gitignore change is untested behavior.

Stale-lock steal has a TOCTOU race: process A stats an old mtime and decides stale, but between stat and unlinkSync another process B may have already stolen and recreated a FRESH lock; A then unlinks B's live lock, allowing a third process to acquire concurrently — two holders, the exact clobber the lock is meant to prevent. Only fires near the 30s threshold and is untested, but it defeats the self-heal guarantee under contention.

The lock only guards upsertTask. lib/archive.mjs:82 does a read-modify-write via store.saveTasks(store.tasks().filter(...)) OUTSIDE the lock. A concurrent `chalk archive` and `upsertTask` still race with last-writer-wins on tasks.json — the criterion's spine-safety goal is not fully met for all mutators, and no test covers archive concurrency.

Test 2 ('no .tmp residue / valid JSON') does not actually exercise the torn-read window: because upsertTask serializes writes under the lock, temp collisions can't occur regardless, and the test never reads tasks.json mid-write. It is a weak proxy for the atomicity claim in criterion 2 (concurrent reader always sees a complete file).

gitignore adds redundant `.chalk/*.tmp.*` but the actual temp name is `tasks.json.<pid>.tmp` (ends in .tmp); the extra pattern is dead. Harmless, but signals the ignore rules weren't verified against the real temp filename.

Criterion 2 (atomic temp+rename writes) is not actually pinned by any locked test. Reverting writeJSON to a plain writeFileSync would leave the entire suite green: test 'atomic writes leave no .tmp residue' asserts stray===[] and valid JSON, but with a plain writeFileSync NO temp file is ever created, so the residue check passes trivially, and after all serialized writes complete the file is valid JSON either way. Because the lock (test 1) serializes writes, a torn-read window never occurs regardless of atomicity, so no assertion distinguishes atomic-write from non-atomic-write. The core behavior of criterion 2 ships without a failing-on-revert test.

Criterion 2 claims 'a concurrent reader always sees a complete valid file', but no test ever spawns a reader that reads tasks.json DURING a write. The atomicity guarantee is asserted only by proxy (post-hoc validity + no residue), neither of which exercises the truncate-then-write window a plain write would expose.

withLock is best-effort: after LOCK_WAIT_MS (15s) it proceeds UNLOCKED (fd===null) and then its finally block does not unlink the lock. Under sustained real contention a process could write outside the lock and still clobber a concurrent upsert — the exact last-writer-wins failure criterion 1 aims to eliminate. Unlikely in the 16-process test (each holds the lock for ms), so untested, but it means criterion 1's guarantee is conditional, not absolute.

writeJSON is now atomic for ALL callers (chalk.json, questions.json, spine.hashes.json, initSpine), broadening the change beyond the stated tasks.json scope. Benign/likely-improving, but it is behavior change to unrelated write paths with no dedicated coverage.

Criterion 3 (lock artifacts stay out of the repo): the stale-steal path renames the lock to `.chalk/.lock.stale.<pid>.<ts>` then unlinks it. That intermediate name matches neither the gitignore entry `.chalk/.lock` (exact) nor `.chalk/*.tmp` (needs .tmp suffix). In the normal path it is unlinked immediately, but a crash between renameSync and unlinkSync would leave an un-gitignored artifact in the repo. Narrow window, not covered by any test.

withLock is best-effort: after LOCK_WAIT_MS (15s) it proceeds UNLOCKED, which under pathological contention could reintroduce the last-writer-wins clobber criterion 1 forbids. This is a deliberate anti-deadlock backstop and is not reachable by the fast operations the tests exercise, so no test covers the unlocked fallback path.

writeJSON is now temp+rename for ALL spine writes (saveMeta, saveQuestions, initSpine, archive files), not just tasks.json. rename(2) requires same-filesystem sibling temp (satisfied here). No behavioral regression observed; sleepSync degrades to a busy-spin only if SharedArrayBuffer is unavailable (documented, functionally harmless).

Tests genuinely exercise the change and are non-vacuous (reverting the lock/atomic-write would fail the concurrent-adds and torn-read tests; reverting the archive routing would leave the stale lock, failing the archive test). All three criteria have direct assertions. Adequate.

title: feat: chalk start refuses a second in-progress task unless protocol.parallel.enabled — make the one-at-a-time convention a hard gate (#110 slice 4)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk start <id> refuses with a non-zero exit when another task is already in-progress and parallel mode is off; the task is NOT moved to in-progress, and the error names the blocking task and how to opt in
With protocol.parallel.enabled=true (or the --parallel flag) chalk start allows a second concurrent in-progress task
The gate does not fire when there is no other in-progress task (starting the first task) nor when re-running start on the already-in-progress task itself

findings.note: Tests cover all three criteria (refuse+no-move+names blocker+parallel word, config opt-in, --parallel flag, first-task and same-task no-fire). Reverting the change fails the suite, so coverage is non-vacuous. Not asserted: the specific '--parallel' text/opt-in phrasing beyond the /parallel/i match, and the multi-blocker count wording — minor gaps only.

Adds a hard gate to the common 'start' path; any pre-existing workflow/test that legitimately started a second task without parallel mode would now break. Scope is intentional per criteria and diff is minimal, so acceptable.

title: feat: chalk pipeline --parallel N — fan out per-task stage chains in worktrees, serialize merges at the gate (#110 slice 3)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk pipeline --parallel N runs up to N per-task chains (branch..evidence) concurrently, each in its own worktree, and never exceeds N in flight; every queued issue-backed task is chained (none dropped by the pool)

Merges are SERIALIZED — never concurrent — because they squash onto the shared base branch and contend; a chain that reaches the merge point cleanly is merged, and the git/gh stage runners are injectable so these invariants are testable without a live repo
A chain that blocks (non-zero) is NOT merged and is reported blocked; a merge that fails blocks that task; the sequential chalk pipeline (no --parallel) path is unchanged

findings.note: Every locked test injects both runChain and runMerge, so the entire DEFAULT production path is untested: defaultRunChain (spawn `pipeline --only <id> --stop-before merge`), defaultRunMerge (`chalk merge`), and the new `stopBefore` truncation in runPipeline. The invariant that chains actually stop before merge and that merges are serialized via a separate `chalk merge` step hinges on stopBefore — yet reverting the stopBefore logic (or having merge run inside the chain) would leave the suite fully green. The real mechanism satisfying criterion 2 has no coverage.

runPipeline's `--only` filter was widened from exact `t.id === only` to `t.id === only || t.id.startsWith(only)`. defaultRunChain passes the FULL id, but with prefix ids like `task-1`/`task-12`, `'task-12'.startsWith('task-1')` is true — so a single chain subprocess would run a sibling task's full chain too. That both double-runs a task and executes work outside the pool, violating criterion 1's 'never exceeds N in flight' and 'each in its own chain/worktree'. Untested.

The same `--only` startsWith broadening changes existing sequential `chalk pipeline --only <id>` behavior (previously exact match) with no test guarding it; any prefix-colliding task ids now get swept into a run that was expected to touch a single task.

Criterion 1 requires each chain run 'in its own worktree,' but defaultRunChain spawns every chain with `cwd: store.root` — a shared checkout — with no worktree creation in this diff. The comment asserts safety 'because of earlier slices,' but nothing here demonstrates per-task worktree isolation; concurrent chains in the same cwd could clobber each other's working tree. This is neither implemented in-diff nor tested.

The CLI wiring in bin/chalk.mjs (--parallel default of 4, `=== true ? 4 : Number()||4` parsing, exit-code 2 on blocked, summary output, and the new top-level `await Promise.resolve(fn())`) is entirely unexercised by tests.

The CLI glue in bin/chalk.mjs (the `--parallel N` flag parsing incl. `--parallel` bare→4 and `Number(flags.parallel)||4` defaulting, the parallel summary line, syncBrowser call, and `process.exit(2)` on blocked) is new behavior with no locked test — the suite exercises runPipelineParallel/runPipeline directly. Core invariants are covered, but the flag-to-function wiring and exit-code contract are unverified.

bin/chalk.mjs now wraps every command dispatch in `await Promise.resolve(fn(parsed))`, converting the whole CLI entrypoint to top-level await. Reviewed as safe (sync throws still propagate inside the try; sync returns unaffected), but it is a broad change touching all commands, not just the pipeline, and no test asserts sync-command error handling is unchanged.

The parallel queue filter (issue && !done && !blocked) ignores inter-task dependency ordering, so `--parallel` can fan out and merge a dependent task before its prerequisite. This mirrors the existing sequential runPipeline behavior (not a regression introduced here) and the slice-3 criteria don't require dep-ordering, but #110's stated dep-honoring semantics are not preserved by the parallel driver.

Per-worktree isolation of concurrent chains and cross-process tasks.json safety are asserted only by comments and depend on prior slices (branch-stage worktreeAdd + spine lock); the locked tests inject runners and thus never exercise real concurrent worktree/spine contention. Verified branch/commit/work stages do target workdir(store,t), so the design holds, but the concurrency-safety of the default production path is untested.

title: fix: vacuous verify trap — empty protocol.verify auto-passes P4; tighten chalk doctor to a blocker (#152)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk doctor reports a blocking 'fail' when protocol.verify has NO configured gate AND there is a runnable task — even if that task HAS a locked test (an empty verify never runs it, so P4 passes vacuously). Softened to 'warn' when an adversarial reviewer gate is configured (the backstop)
The empty-verify blocker makes chalk doctor exit non-zero and autopilot (which gates on doctor 'fail' findings) refuse — so an unattended run cannot silently complete tasks against a vacuous verify
A project WITH a configured protocol.verify gate produces no such finding (no false positive); the check is independent of the existing per-task testless check

findings.note: The new 'gates' blocker fires for ANY project with a runnable task and an empty protocol.verify (default `chalk init` without a preset). This is the intended behavior per #152, and the one affected editable test (pipeline.test.mjs) was updated to add a verify command, but the change now makes doctor exit non-zero for any bare-init repo with a runnable task — worth confirming no other suites depend on the old permissive doctor. verify is the gate for that.

verifyConfigured uses normGate(v).cmd across Object.values(proto.verify); an entry with an empty command string ({test:''}) is correctly treated as vacuous, and a configured {test:'...'} correctly suppresses the finding. Locked tests cover fail (no reviewer), warn (reviewer backstop), no-false-positive (configured verify), independence from the testless check, and real-CLI non-zero exit — all three criteria are asserted and autopilot gates on level:'fail'. Adequate.

title: fix: review diff-capture silently passes on no diff — abort loudly instead of a vacuous verdict (#151)

acceptanceCriteria.text: When captureDiff yields an empty diff (all git-diff strategies produce nothing), runReview returns a distinct 'no-diff' status WITHOUT invoking the reviewer — so no pass/block verdict is fabricated over an empty change set
chalk review aborts LOUDLY on the no-diff status: non-zero exit, a clear 'no diff captured' message, and NO passing review recorded on the task; it is not treated as a retryable transient error
A review WITH a real (non-empty) diff is unaffected — the normal pass/block flow and recorded verdict are unchanged

findings.note: The EMPTY_TREE (git diff <empty-tree> HEAD) fallback means no-diff is only reachable when HEAD is unborn/no commits; any repo with committed content will render the whole tree as additions rather than aborting. This is intentional and is in fact locked by test 2 (a committed change with no base delta MUST be reviewed, not aborted), so it aligns with the contract — but it does narrow the abort to the unborn-HEAD case, so a misconfigured base on a populated branch reviews the whole repo instead of aborting. Acceptable given the locked spec, noting the boundary.

Criterion 1's no-diff abort is exercised only via the unborn-HEAD path (nothing committed). There is no locked test for an empty base-delta on a branch that already has committed content, but test 2 deliberately locks that case as 'review, not abort', so the coverage is consistent with intended behavior rather than a gap.

title: fix: merge ff-pull failure strands a stale base — chalk branch cuts from the fresh remote base (#150)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk branch cuts the new worktree from the FRESH remote base (origin/<base>, after a best-effort fetch), so a stale local base (behind the remote — e.g. after a failed merge pull --ff-only) does not strand the next task on old code: the worktree contains the remote's latest commits

When origin/<base> cannot be resolved (no remote / offline), branch falls back to the local base and warns if a remote exists — never SILENTLY stale; the merge itself still completes (remote is source of truth), unchanged
The normal case (local base already current) is unaffected — the worktree is cut from the base tip as before

findings.note: Criterion 2's second clause — 'when origin/<base> cannot be resolved but a remote EXISTS, fall back to local base AND warn (never silently stale)' — is entirely untested. Test 2 covers only the NO-remote case (currentRepo null, no warning). The 'remote exists but unresolvable/offline' branch (the console.warn on line 376) has no assertion; reverting that line/branch leaves both locked tests green. That is a vacuous pass for the warning contract.

The warning is gated on currentRepo(s.root), which only detects a parseable origin remote URL. A remote under a non-'origin' name, or an origin URL that fails the owner/repo regex, yields currentRepo()===null → no warning → SILENTLY stale, contradicting criterion 2's 'warns if a remote exists'. A `git remote` presence check would match the criterion more faithfully.

`fetch origin ${base}` is best-effort (swallowed by gitTry) but no test forces the fetch-fails-yet-origin-ref-exists path; if a prior stale origin/<base> ref exists and fetch fails, branch would cut from a stale remote-tracking ref while reporting success — a plausible edge not exercised.

branch now always cuts from origin/<base> when it resolves. If the local base were ever AHEAD of the remote (unpushed local commits on main), the new worktree would silently drop them. Chalk's workflow never commits to main locally (all work goes through PR worktrees), so this is out of scope of the criteria, but it is an unguarded edge the tests don't cover.

`git fetch origin ${base}` and `rev-parse origin/${base}` interpolate the configured base unquoted (worktreeAdd itself quotes via q()). Harmless for the canonical 'main' base and consistent with the spec's origin/<base> wording, but a base value with shell-special chars would be mishandled.

Test 3 (no remote) would still pass if the change were reverted (fileA is present with or without the patch). The load-bearing coverage comes from Test 1 (fileB recovery fails on revert) and Test 2 (warning fails on revert), which do pin the added behavior — so the suite is not vacuous, but the no-remote path is only weakly asserted (no explicit assertion that NO warning/fetch-crash occurs).

title: feat: spine/protocol migration — stamp writer version, detect skew on open, gated chalk migrate (#159)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Opening a spine stamped with a NEWER chalk-protocol version than the running binary is REFUSED (non-zero exit, a clear upgrade message) rather than silently misread — enforced in Store.open via spineSkew

chalk migrate carries an old-schema spine (pre-writerVersion, version 1.0) forward to the current schema, is IDEMPOTENT on re-run (no-op when already current), backs the spine up before mutating, and --dry-run mutates NOTHING
A same-version (compatible) spine opens with NO migration prompt and NO mutation of chalk.json; chalk doctor surfaces skew (fail for newer, warn for needs-migrate)

findings.note: doctor.mjs adds a `fail`-for-newer branch, but it is effectively unreachable via the CLI: Store.open throws on a newer spine before runDoctor executes. The locked test even acknowledges this ('the refusal IS the surfaced skew'). Reverting just that doctor line would leave all tests green — it is untested defensive code. Criterion 3's literal 'chalk doctor surfaces skew (fail for newer)' is satisfied only by the open-time refusal, not by doctor itself.

saveMeta now stamps writerVersion (and continues to bump updatedAt) on EVERY spine write, and initSpine bumps version 1.0→1.1. Any existing test or consumer doing an exact-snapshot deepEqual of chalk.json could break; the visible suite does not appear to, but the held-out set cannot be inspected. Low risk given the field is additive.

Store.open is now invoked twice per command (warnSpineTamper swallows the newer-skew throw, then the command handler re-throws it). Behavior is correct — the refusal still surfaces with exit 1 and the writer version — but the swallow in warnSpineTamper means the skew guard relies on the command handler also calling Store.open; a command that skips Store.open would bypass the refusal. All tested commands (status/doctor/migrate) do call it.

title: feat: chalk stats --public — PII-free shareable gate-efficacy artifact (markdown + shields badge) (#156)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk stats --public emits a PII-FREE markdown summary carrying the efficacy headline (gate catches, tasks reviewed, block/pass verdicts, self-report↔gate disagreement rate, verify-RED blocks, gated-landing fraction, held-out audit) and NO task titles, file paths, or task ids (the churn 'worst' list is excluded)
chalk stats --badge emits shields.io endpoint JSON (schemaVersion, label, message = the gate-catch count, color); chalk stats --public --json emits the PII-free summary object
The public summary is derived purely from computeStats (deterministic for a fixed spine fixture, no new tracking) and README documents it as the social-proof block

findings.note: disagreementRate is defined as caught/reviewed (blocked-then-passed), which understates true self-report↔gate disagreement: a task blocked but not yet re-passed is a disagreement yet is excluded. Matches the locked test's 50% expectation, so acceptable, but the label 'disagreement' is a slight approximation.

The 'held-out audit', 'verify-RED blocks', and 'gated-landing fraction' rows of the markdown headline (criterion 1) are rendered but not directly asserted by any test; they would still render if their values were wrong. Core headline fields (catches, reviewed, verdicts, disagreement) and PII-absence are well covered.
An undocumented --md flag path was added alongside --public/--badge; harmless but not part of the stated contract and untested.

title: feat: chalk feedback --submit — upstream feedback via prefilled GitHub issue URL (#157)

acceptanceCriteria.text: buildUpstreamFeedbackUrl is a pure function that percent-encodes the message into a GitHub new-issue URL for the configured upstream repo, including the chalk version in the body and a user-feedback label

chalk feedback --submit "<msg>" prints that URL, works with NO .chalk spine (runs before Store.open), and never creates .chalk/feedback or calls the analysis agent; CHALK_UPSTREAM_REPO overrides the repo
The existing chalk feedback signal-analysis path is unchanged; an empty --submit message is a clear usage error

findings.note: Criterion 3's 'existing signal-analysis path unchanged' is not directly asserted in feedback-submit.test.mjs, but the new behavior is fully guarded by an early return on `flags.submit !== undefined` so the legacy path is structurally untouched; low regression risk.

Empty-message path relies on `_.slice(1).join(' ')` where `_` includes the 'feedback' command token; verified the arg parser (line 58) returns the full positional array and the dispatch passes it through, so slice(1) correctly drops the command name and yields '' → clean die(). Behaves as tested.

title: feat: package-update handling — fix --version, opt-out update notifier, chalk upgrade (#158)

acceptanceCriteria.text: chalk --version and -v print the installed PACKAGE version (semver) and exit 0; chalk version still prints only the protocol tag (chalk/0) for scripts that parse it

The update notifier is skip-FIRST and inert in every non-interactive/opted-out context (non-TTY, --json, CI, CHALK_NO_UPDATE_CHECK, protocol.updateCheck:false), prefers a fresh once-per-day cache over the network, and a registry failure never throws or changes a command's exit code / --json output
chalk upgrade [--dry-run] prints (or runs) the correct global-npm update command

findings.note: Criterion 3 (`chalk upgrade [--dry-run]`) is entirely untested. The locked suite (test/update-notifier.test.mjs) covers only --version/-v and the update notifier; nothing spawns `chalk upgrade` or `chalk upgrade --dry-run` to assert it prints/runs `npm i -g chalk-protocol@latest`. The whole `upgrade` command could be deleted and the suite would still pass — a vacuous pass for an entire acceptance criterion. Per the hard rule, this alone requires block. A trivial `run('upgrade','--dry-run')` assertion on the printed command is missing.

The notifier's INTERACTIVE path (isTTY true, no opt-out) is never exercised end-to-end through bin/chalk.mjs. Every spawned test runs non-TTY, so the actual wiring in bin (the `process.stdout.isTTY && parsed.flags.json !== true` guard, config read via Store, console.error emission) is never asserted to emit a notice; only the pure lib functions are unit-tested. A regression that broke the bin-level invocation would go undetected.

`chalk upgrade` runs `npm i -g chalk-protocol@latest` unconditionally with no confirmation and inherits stdio; combined with being untested, a broken/incorrect command string would ship silently. Also the notifier reads protocol().updateCheck but the env-vs-config precedence (e.g., CHALK_NO_UPDATE_CHECK vs updateCheck:true) is only validated in isolation, not as composed in bin.

The interactive bin display path (process.stdout.isTTY branch that actually console.error's the notice) is never exercised end-to-end — TTY can't easily be faked in spawnSync tests. The risky guarantees (inertness under non-TTY/--json, no exit-code/JSON pollution) ARE covered via bin, and checkForUpdate's notice production is unit-tested, so this is thin untested glue rather than an uncovered feature. Reverting the bin diff would fail the --version/upgrade/inertness tests, so the suite is not vacuous.
chalk upgrade non-dry-run (execSync 'npm i -g chalk-protocol@latest') is unavoidably untested; acceptable since criterion 3 is satisfied via the dry-run path and the command string is asserted.

Interactive notifier awaits checkForUpdate (up to a 1.5s network timeout) before running the actual command, adding latency to every interactive invocation; bounded and TTY-only, so within stated design but worth noting.

title: docs: document the promote CI-poll knobs (ciPollIntervalMs/ciPollAttempts) in CONFIG + a runtime hint (#153)

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/CONFIG.md's github section DESCRIBES ciPollIntervalMs and ciPollAttempts — what they control (remote-CI polling cadence during merge/promote) and the ciPollAttempts:0 never-wait escape — not merely lists them; the docs/CONFIG.md ↔ initSpine drift gate stays green
The release --promote CI-poll timeout (still-pending) message points the user at github.ciPollAttempts / ciPollIntervalMs

findings.note: docs/CONFIG.md's new text claims `ciPollAttempts: 0` means "never wait (fall straight through to local verify)". This misdescribes the actual behavior. In lib/brokecheck.mjs, when the PR HAS pending checks, maxAttempts=0 skips the poll loop and returns ci==='pending' → {ok:false, 'remote CI still running — merge when checks complete'}, i.e. a hard block, NOT a fall-through to local verify. Local verify only runs when ci==='none' (no checks at all), which is independent of ciPollAttempts. Worse, in the promote path (bin/chalk.mjs:730) 0 with pending CI dies with 'still pending after 0 check(s)' — never local verify. A user setting 0 to bypass slow CI would instead get an immediate block, the opposite of what the docs promise. This defeats criterion 1's requirement to accurately DESCRIBE the never-wait escape.

The locked test only asserts the literal presence of /ciPollAttempts: ?0|never wait/ in the github section; it never validates that the described 0-behavior matches the code. The inaccurate '(fall straight through to local verify)' parenthetical therefore passes unchecked — the test would stay green even with a wrong description.

Criterion 1: docs/CONFIG.md github section now describes ciPollIntervalMs/ciPollAttempts (polling cadence, defaults 5000/24≈2min, applies to merge and release --promote) and the ciPollAttempts:0 never-wait escape. The 0-behavior claim ('blocks a still-pending check, does NOT fall back to local verify') is accurate against brokeCheck (loop skipped with maxAttempts=0 → ci stays 'pending' → returns ok:false source:'ci' without calling verifyFn). Fallback-to-local only for 'none'. Verified correct.

Criterion 2: the release --promote still-pending die() message now names github.ciPollAttempts / ciPollIntervalMs, and locked test 4 asserts /still pending[\s\S]{0,120}ciPollAttempts/ against bin/chalk.mjs — reverting the message change would fail this test. The doc drift gate, description, and 0-escape accuracy are each pinned by their own locked assertions. Change is non-vacuously covered.

title: docs: embed the cheat-caught demo GIF in the "Does the gate actually catch anything?" README section

acceptanceCriteria.text: README.md embeds docs/assets/cheat-caught.gif inside the "Does the gate actually catch anything?" section using a relative ./docs/assets/ path
The embed has descriptive alt text and a one-line caption naming what it shows (an agent gaming its locked test; the gate refusing)

findings.note: The diff under review is enormous (30+ files: migrate, pipeline --parallel, stats --public, feedback --submit, etc.) for a docs-only task. This violates the 'keep diffs small and scoped' rule. However, the two acceptance criteria concern only the README embed, and those unrelated changes appear to be pre-existing branch commits carrying their own tests — they don't undermine the docs criteria.

Both criteria are verifiably met: README.md line 112 embeds ./docs/assets/cheat-caught.gif via a relative ./docs/assets/ path; the alt text describes an agent gaming its locked test and chalk done refusing; line 114 is a one-line italic caption naming what it shows. The referenced GIF file exists on disk (docs/assets/cheat-caught.gif). A docs-content criterion is not automatable, so absence of a test is not a vacuous-pass violation here.
